items.title: HaloMD faces lawsuit alleging No Surprises Act middleman used ‘sham letter,’ misleading data - statnews.com
items.reason: This item directly addresses No Surprises Act litigation involving alleged violations of the law's protections, specifically concerning an IDR intermediary's conduct and misrepresentation.
items.summary: HaloMD, a No Surprises Act Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) middleman, faces a lawsuit alleging it used fraudulent documentation and misleading data in violation of the Act's balance-billing protections. The complaint suggests HaloMD improperly issued communications and data representations that circumvented No Surprises Act safeguards designed to protect patients and providers from surprise bills. This litigation is significant for revenue-cycle teams as it tests enforcement of IDR process integrity and highlights potential vulnerabilities in third-party intermediary conduct, which directly impacts how disputed claims are resolved and how balance-billing protections are enforced in practice.

items.title: Federal Independent Dispute Resolution Operations
items.reason: This Federal Register document directly addresses final rules for the federal IDR process under the No Surprises Act, including operational requirements, administrative fee structures, batching rules, eligibility determinations, and dispute initiation procedures—all core components of the topic definition.
items.summary: The Federal Register published final rules establishing operational requirements for the federal Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) process under the No Surprises Act. The rules mandate that group health plans and health insurance issuers disclose specific information with initial payment or denial notices, communicate using standardized claim adjustment reason codes (CARCs) and remittance advice remark codes (RARCs), and establish procedures for the open negotiation period, IDR initiation, eligibility review, and administrative/certified IDR entity fee collection. The rules also define bundled payment arrangements, amend batching requirements, and clarify timeframe extensions for extenuating circumstances. These operational changes directly affect how out-of-network disputes are processed and arbitrated, impacting revenue-cycle teams' compliance obligations and dispute resolution procedures.

items.title: Federal IDR fee drops from $115 to $15: 6 things spine and orthopedic leaders should know - beckersspine.com
items.reason: The item directly addresses a material change to IDR administrative fees, which is explicitly within the topic scope as a certified-IDRE fee change affecting the federal IDR process.
items.summary: The federal IDR administrative fee has been reduced from $115 to $15 per dispute, a significant change to the cost structure of the Independent Dispute Resolution process under the No Surprises Act. This fee reduction directly impacts healthcare providers and payers who utilize the IDR process to resolve out-of-network payment disputes, potentially increasing the volume of disputes filed due to lower barriers to entry. Revenue-cycle teams should expect increased dispute activity and adjust their processes and budgets accordingly to account for the dramatically lower cost of initiating IDR cases.

also.title: Lower No Surprises Act dispute fees officially go into effect - Radiology Business
